title: Serializing and Serving the Model
concept: The final tuned model gets saved to a file so it can be loaded anywhere, and the support dashboard calls a prediction API to fetch a live churn-risk score for each customer, while StreamBox keeps watching for drift that could make the saved model stale.

exercises.prompt: Recap: StreamBox picked a decision threshold of 0.3 because a missed churner costs about 20 times more than an unnecessary discount. What's left before this model can actually help support agents day to day?

options.text: The trained model needs to be saved to a file and connected to the tools the support team actually uses
Nothing — a trained model automatically appears inside every app the company owns
The model needs to be retrained completely from scratch every single day

exercises.explain: A model sitting inside a training notebook doesn't help anyone yet — it has to be saved and plugged into real tools.

cards.title: From notebook to dashboard
cards.body: Right now, StreamBox's tuned Random Forest only exists inside the data scientist's training session — powerful, but trapped. The support team, who actually talks to at-risk customers, can't use it at all yet.

cards.title: Serializing the final model
cards.body: StreamBox serializes the tuned Random Forest — saving its trees, splits, and settings to a file called churn_model_v1.pkl — freezing it exactly as it finished training, ready to be loaded anywhere without retraining.

cards.title: Shipping a finished product
cards.body: Training a model is like assembling a product in a workshop. Serialization is boxing it up so it can be shipped anywhere — the support dashboard, a mobile app, or a nightly batch job — and unpacked in perfect working condition.

cards.title: The support dashboard's live lookup
cards.body: When a support agent opens customer #48213's profile, the dashboard sends that customer's features to a churn-prediction API. The loaded model scores them in milliseconds and returns a churn-risk score — say 0.82, or 82% — which the dashboard shows as a red "High Risk" badge.

cards.title: Serving a model isn't a one-time task
cards.body: It's easy to assume that once a model is saved and connected, the job is done forever. In reality, StreamBox must keep watching for drift — like a hit new show shifting everyone's watch_hours patterns — since a model trained on old behavior can quietly get worse at spotting tomorrow's churners.

cards.title: Training Once, Serving Continuously
cards.body: Two very different phases of the model's life:

left.text:
Tune on historical data
Pick the best settings
Save to churn_model_v1.pkl

right.text:
Dashboard calls the API
Get a live risk score
Watch for drift over time

exercises.prompt: What does serializing StreamBox's final Random Forest actually accomplish?

options.text: It saves the fully-trained model's trees and settings to a file, so it can be loaded and reused without retraining
It deletes the model in order to save disk space
It converts the trained model back into raw, unprocessed training data

exercises.explain: Serialization freezes the trained model's state into a file, ready for instant reuse anywhere it's needed.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The customer support dashboard needs to understand exactly how the Random Forest's 200 trees vote in order to display a churn-risk score.
exercises.explain: False. The dashboard just calls the prediction API with a customer's data and receives a risk score back — it doesn't need to know anything about the model's internal workings.

exercises.sentence: Even after StreamBox's model is deployed, the team must keep monitoring for ___, since real customer behavior can shift and make an old model less accurate.
exercises.explain: Drift — a change in incoming data patterns or in the relationship between features and churn — is exactly what ongoing monitoring after deployment is meant to catch.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the best sign that StreamBox's deployed churn model might be suffering from drift and need retraining?

options.text: Average watch_hours and support_ticket patterns among current customers look noticeably different than they did when the model was trained
The support dashboard's loading spinner takes two extra seconds to appear
The model file's name still says v1 after several months

exercises.explain: A real shift in the incoming data's patterns compared to training time is the classic signature of drift.

exercises.prompt: StreamBox saves its tuned Random Forest to churn_model_v1.pkl and wires it into the support dashboard's prediction API. Six months later, StreamBox launches a wildly popular new show that changes how almost everyone uses the app. What should StreamBox do, and why?

options.text: Monitor for data drift, and if customer behavior patterns have meaningfully shifted, retrain the model on fresh data so its churn predictions stay accurate
Nothing — once a model is deployed and serving predictions, it stays accurate forever
Immediately delete the model, since any change in behavior means it's now completely useless
Simply rename the file to churn_model_v2.pkl without changing anything else about it

exercises.explain: Exactly! Deployment isn't the finish line — it's the start of an ongoing cycle of monitoring, and retraining when the world genuinely changes. You now know the full lifecycle of a real machine learning system, end to end!
